questions.question: Pitch is the human perception of a sound's:
questions.explanation: Pitch is how high or low a sound seems to a listener — it is determined by the frequency of the sound wave.

questions.question: A sound with a high pitch has a:
questions.explanation: High pitch corresponds to high frequency. Low pitch corresponds to low frequency.

questions.question: The audible frequency range for humans is approximately:
questions.explanation: Humans can typically hear frequencies from $20\text{ Hz}$ to $20\text{ kHz}$. Frequencies outside this range are inaudible.

questions.question: Infrasound refers to sound frequencies:
questions.explanation: Infrasound has frequencies below the human hearing threshold of $20\text{ Hz}$. Sources include earthquakes and large machinery.

questions.question: Ultrasound refers to sound frequencies:
questions.explanation: Ultrasound has frequencies above $20\text{ kHz}$, beyond human hearing. It is used in medical imaging and by bats for echolocation.

questions.question: Increasing the tension in a guitar string causes the pitch to:
questions.explanation: Higher tension increases the frequency of vibration, producing a higher pitch.

questions.question: A shorter guitar string (fretted) produces a:
options: Lower pitch than a longer string
Higher pitch than a longer string
Pitch equal to a longer string
questions.explanation: Shorter strings vibrate at higher frequencies, producing higher pitches. This is why pressing a string against a fret raises the note.

questions.question: A thick guitar string produces a:
options: Higher pitch than a thin string
Lower pitch than a thin string
Pitch equal to a thin string
questions.explanation: Thicker strings vibrate more slowly (lower frequency), producing a lower pitch than thinner strings under the same tension.

questions.question: A sound wave has a period of $0.005\text{ s}$. What is its frequency?
A sound wave has a frequency of $50\text{ Hz}$. What is its period?
Which animal can detect ultrasound frequencies above the human range?
questions.explanation: Bats use ultrasound for echolocation, emitting frequencies up to $100\text{ kHz}$ — well above human hearing. Elephants and baleen whales communicate using infrasound.

questions.question: Which of the following is a natural source of infrasound?
options: A flute playing a high note
questions.explanation: Earthquakes produce powerful infrasound waves below $20\text{ Hz}$. A mosquito's wings and a flute produce audible frequencies; a dog whistle produces ultrasound.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered pitch, frequency, and the factors affecting them!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the effects of tension, length, and thickness on the pitch of stringed instruments.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the frequency ranges for infrasound, audible sound, and ultrasound.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: pitch = perception of frequency, $f = 1/T$, and the human hearing range.
